facts_disclosed: Extension officer spacing advice ruined 2 hectares, 3m too close vs father's 4m

turns.content: i need help my friend. i have 2 hectares of new cocoa planting and the trees are not growing well. they look weak and thin

Sorry to hear that. Weak growth in new plantings can come from several things -- soil quality, water, shade management, or spacing issues. How old are these trees, and what does the weakness look like exactly? Yellowing leaves, thin trunks, poor canopy spread?
planted last year. the trunks are thin yes. leaves are small and some yellowing at the edges. they do not fill out like I expected

Thin trunks and small yellowing leaves after a full year in the ground is concerning. That combination usually points to competition -- the trees are fighting for something they're not getting enough of.

Let me ask a few things:
1. What spacing did you plant at?
2. Do the new trees have shade from plantain or other nurse crops?
3. Is the soil the same as your other cocoa plots?
4. Have you applied fertilizer since planting?
the soil is the same type as my other plots. I applied fertilizer at planting time. the shade trees are there. the problem is spacing my friend
If the soil, shade, and fertilizer are all in order, then spacing is very likely the culprit. What distance did you plant at?
the extension officer told us to plant at 3 metre spacing. too close my friend. my 2 hectares are suffering because the trees compete for light and everything else

3 metre spacing is quite tight for cocoa. At 3m x 3m, each tree only has about 9 square metres to work with. Once the canopy starts to develop -- which happens fast in the first year or two -- the trees begin shading each other out and the root systems overlap, competing for water and nutrients.

The thin trunks and small leaves you're seeing are classic signs of overcrowding. The trees are putting energy into growing upward to find light instead of developing strong trunks and full canopy.

What spacing did your older, healthier plots use?
my father always planted at 4 metres. every tree had room to breathe and grow properly. he farmed cocoa for over 40 years and never changed from 4 metres. he was right

Your father's 4 metre spacing gives each tree roughly 16 square metres -- nearly double what they get at 3m. That extra room makes a huge difference for canopy development, root spread, and ultimately pod production.

The extension officers often recommend 3m because it puts more trees per hectare -- about 1,111 versus 625 at 4m. The theory is more trees means more cocoa. But that only works if every tree can develop fully, which doesn't happen when they're crowded.

Sometimes what works on a research station doesn't translate to real farm conditions.
exactly. sometimes book knowledge is not farm knowledge. the research station has perfect conditions. irrigation, perfect soil, everything controlled. our farms are not like that

You've hit on an important point. Research station results come from ideal conditions -- controlled irrigation, prepared soil, precise fertilizer schedules. Real farms deal with variable rainfall, natural soil differences, and limited inputs. Trees planted too close on a research station might survive because everything else is optimized. On a working farm, that margin disappears.

For your 2 hectares, you have a few options to fix this:

1. **Thin every other row** -- remove alternate trees to open up space immediately
2. **Selective thinning** -- keep the strongest trees and remove the weakest regardless of position
3. **Heavy pruning** -- don't remove trees but prune aggressively to reduce canopy competition

Option 1 or 2 will give the remaining trees the best chance. Option 3 is a half-measure that delays the problem.
thinning is painful. each seedling cost money and labour to plant. but if I leave them crowded they all suffer

That's the difficult truth. Keeping them all means none of them reach their potential. The money spent on those seedlings is already gone -- the question now is whether you protect the investment in the remaining trees or watch all of them underperform for years.

If you thin every other row, you'd go from roughly 1,111 trees per hectare to about 556 -- still a productive density, and each remaining tree would have roughly 3m x 6m of space. Not the same as 4x4, but a big improvement.

The trees you remove don't have to be wasted either. Can you transplant the healthiest ones if you have any open land?
no open land for transplanting. I would cut them and leave as mulch. obi nnim whether that is the right choice but I think thinning every other row is the only real solution

Leaving them as mulch is actually a good use -- the decomposing wood and leaves return nutrients to the soil and help retain moisture for the remaining trees. So the removed trees still contribute something.

I think thinning is the right call. When would you plan to do it? Dry season is usually best for this kind of work -- less risk of fungal disease entering the disturbed soil.
dry season yes. Kwabena will help me. 2 hectares will take maybe 4 days of hard work to mark and remove

4 days with help sounds about right. A few tips for when you do it:

- **Mark first, cut second** -- walk the rows and tie a ribbon or cloth on the trees you're removing. Step back and check the spacing looks right before you cut anything.
- **Keep the strongest** -- if two neighbouring trees are different sizes, always remove the weaker one even if it breaks the strict alternating pattern.
- **Cut at ground level** and treat the stump with wood ash to discourage disease.
- **Spread the cut material** as mulch around the base of the trees you're keeping.

Do you want to tell the extension officer about the results, or just handle it yourself?
I will handle it myself. the other farmers who followed the same advice are also seeing problems. I will tell them what I am doing so they can decide for themselves

That's good leadership -- sharing what you've learned so others can make informed decisions. If several farmers are seeing the same problem at 3m spacing, that's real evidence from real farms, not a research station.

Did many farmers in the area follow the same 3m advice?

maybe 10 or 12 farmers planted new plots at 3 metres because the officer recommended it. some are seeing the same thing I see. the trees are thin and fighting each other. my father's way was better my friend

10 or 12 farmers all seeing the same result confirms it's the spacing, not something specific to your soil or plot. Your father's 40 years of experience at 4 metres produced strong evidence -- the older trees on your farm are the proof.

When you talk to the other farmers, they can look at your older plots versus the new 3m planting and see the difference with their own eyes. That's more convincing than any advice.
yes. the proof is there on the farm for anyone who wants to see. Nyame willing the remaining trees will recover after thinning. medaase my friend for the help

Nyame willing, they will. Cocoa trees are resilient when they have room. Once you thin and give them space, you should see stronger growth within one season.

Good luck with the planning, and I hope the other farmers benefit from what you share. Take care.
